introduction: Ascending pelvic infection involving the upper genital tract. Usually involves sexually transmitted organisms e.g. Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis. It may also be caused by organisms endogenous to the lower genital tract. In severe cases, organisms may migrate via the peritoneum to the upper abdomen causing perihepatic adhesions: the so-called "violin strings" (Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome).
investigations: Midstream urine microscopy, culture and sensitivity
High vaginal swab culture: to exclude trichomoniasis, bacterial vaginosis
Ultrasound scan: to exclude cyesis, ectopic gestation, adnexal mass (e.g. ovarian mass)

drug: Ceftriaxone (or equivalent cephalosporin): 1 g intramuscularly stat
Doxycycline 100 mg orally every 12 hours for 14 days
Metronidazole: 400 mg orally every 12 hours for 14 days
Metronidazole 400 mg orally every 8 hours for 10-14 days
supportive_measures: Admit, re-evaluate and give appropriate intravenous therapy

prevention: Encourage the use of barrier contraceptive with spermicides
Modify risky sexual behaviour: avoid multiplicity of sexual partners
Contact tracing: to break the existing chain of infection and prevent recurrence
Prompt diagnosis and treatment to prevent long-term complications
